coredata.dc:title: Color-tunable fluorescent nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots derived from pineapple leaf fiber biomass to detect Hg2+
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, CAS.
coredata.prism:publisher: Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, CAS.
coredata.dc:creator: XIONG, Zeng-Heng
ZOU, Ya-Nan
CAO, Xiao-Cong
LIN, Zhao-Hua
coredata.dc:description: In this study, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) exhibiting high water solubility were produced based on the hydrothermal approach. Ethylenediamine (EDA) was used as the nitrogen dopant and microcrystalline cellulose (MCC), extracted from pineapple leaf fiber (PALF) biomass, used as the carbon source. The as-prepared N-GQDs exhibited an average size of 3.7 nm, 8-10 layers and the excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence. It was noteworthy that controlling the pH value of the EDA solution in the hydrothermal process could change the fluorescence color of the N-GQDs, exhibiting unique green, cyan and blue light. Besides, because of the effective fluorescence quenching effect of N-GQDs by mercury (II) ions (Hg2+), the as-prepared N-GQDs were successfully employed as the efficient nanosized fluorescence sensors to selectively detect Hg2+ in aqueous solutions with the detection limit of 0.25 nM and a linear range of 0∼30 μM. The selectivity experiments indicated that as-prepared N-GQDs were specific for Hg2+ even with interference by high concentrations of other metal ions. Notably, the N-GQDs could be well adopted to determine Hg2+ in river water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: graphene quantum dots
Pineapple leaf fiber
Ethylenediamine
Fluorescence
Sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: MCC extraction and hydrothermal carbon synthesis
Detection of Hg2+ in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge financial support from the Scientific Research Foundation of Higher Education Institutions, Hainan (Hnky2019-98). The authors appreciate the Analytical and Testing Center of Hainan University and Tianhe Research Analysis and Testing Center in terms of their technical supports and instruction.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic illustration of synthesis approach of N-GQDs samples.

SEM images of (a) PALF, (b) MCC and (c) HTC. (d)(e) TEM and HRTEM images of the N-GQDs, respectively. Inset of (d) was particle size distribution of N-GQDs. (f) AFM image (insets: height profile following the distribution of the height as well as the white line).
(a) FT-IR spectra of MCC, HTC and N-GQDs; (b) XRD patterns of HTC and N-GQDs; (c) Raman spectra of MCC, HTCs and N-GQDs; (d) TG curves of HTC and N-GQDs.
(a) XPS survey spectrums of HTC and N-GQDs; (b) XPS C1s spectra of HTC and N-GQDs.

(a) PL emission spectra and (b) the photoluminescence (PLE) excitation spectra of N-GQDs. (c) The results belonging to the PL and PLE wavelength acquired from (a) and (b). Inset: Photograph of the N-GQDs aqueous solution taken based on maximum excitation wavelength. (d) CIE 1931 coordinates of N-GQDs from PL emission spectra at the excitation wavelength (white dots). (e) PL emission spectra of N-GQDs excited under a wide range of wavelengths by hydrothermal under pH=14 EDA solution. (f) Time-resolved PL spectra of N-GQDs.

(a) Quenching factors (F0- F)/F0, in which F and F0 indicated N-GQDs fluorescence intensity with and without 50 μM Hg2+ .F0 and F were the fluorescence emission intensities of N-GQDs at 438 nm with and without Hg2+. (b) Fluorescence emission spectrum belonging to N-GQDs under Hg2+ with a range of concentrations (from up to bottom: 0, 0.2, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 1, 3, 5, 7, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45, 50, 55, 60 μM). (c) The dependence of PL intensity on Hg2+ concentration ranging from 0–60 μM. The fitted curve shows a linear relationship of Hg2+ concentration and PL intensity. (d) The fluorescence images illustrating the N-GQDs aqueous solution based on UV irradiation excited at 365 nm.
The content of each component elements of HTC and N-GQDs.
Comparison of different types of quantum dot fluorescent sensors for Hg2+ detection.
Results of the accuracy and precision studies of river water samples spiked with Hg2+ (n=6).

head.ce:title: Color-tunable fluorescent nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots derived from pineapple leaf fiber biomass to detect Hg2+

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hainan Ecological Environmental Monitoring Center, 98 Baiju Avenue, Haikou 571126, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hainan Technology and Business College, 2 Wentan Avenue, Haikou 571100, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bHainan Technology and Business College, 2 Wentan Avenue, Haikou 571100, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hainan Research Academy of Environmental Sciences, 98 Baiju Avenue, Haikou 571126, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hainan Research Academy of Environmental Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cHainan Research Academy of Environmental Sciences, 98 Baiju Avenue, Haikou 571126, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Advanced Materials of Tropical Island Resources of Ministry of Education, College of Chemical Engineering, Hainan University, Haikou 570228, Hainan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Advanced Materials of Tropical Island Resources of Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: dKey Laboratory of Advanced Materials of Tropical Island Resources of Ministry of Education, College of Chemical Engineering, Hainan University, Haikou 570228, Hainan, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) exhibiting high water solubility were produced based on the hydrothermal approach. Ethylenediamine (EDA) was used as the nitrogen dopant and microcrystalline cellulose (MCC), extracted from pineapple leaf fiber (PALF) biomass, used as the carbon source. The as-prepared N-GQDs exhibited an average size of 3.7 nm, 8-10 layers and the excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence. It was noteworthy that controlling the pH value of the EDA solution in the hydrothermal process could change the fluorescence color of the N-GQDs, exhibiting unique green, cyan and blue light. Besides, because of the effective fluorescence quenching effect of N-GQDs by mercury (II) ions (Hg2+), the as-prepared N-GQDs were successfully employed as the efficient nanosized fluorescence sensors to selectively detect Hg2+ in aqueous solutions with the detection limit of 0.25 nM and a linear range of 0∼30 μM. The selectivity experiments indicated that as-prepared N-GQDs were specific for Hg2+ even with interference by high concentrations of other metal ions. Notably, the N-GQDs could be well adopted to determine Hg2+ in river water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are the graphene sheets with a thickness of lower than 10 layers and the lateral dimensions of less than 100 nm, which have strong quantum constraints and edge effects, thus having special fluorescence properties [1,2]. As impacted by the small toxicity, suitable biocompatibility or high surface area, as well as the availability of a π-π conjugated network with functionalize surface group, GQDs hold great promise for applications such as bio-imaging [3–5], sensors [4–12] and light-emitting diodes [13,14].

Recently, different methods have been developed to produce GQDs, classified into "top-down" and "bottom-up" approaches. The "top-down" method involves splitting up carbonaceous materials (e.g., hydrothermal [15], electrochemical [16], ultrasonicating [17] and acid treatments [18] approaches). Nevertheless, the mentioned approaches adopt expensive non-renewable raw material (e.g., fullerene [19], graphite or graphene oxides and its derivatives [15,20], carbon nanotubes [21] and carbon fibers [22]). Besides, the strong and well-ordered structure exhibited by graphene-based precursors needs special high-pressure/high temperature device and strong acid treatment to decompose in tiny dots. However, in the "bottom-up" approach, GQDs are produced by the step-wise chemical fusion of various aromatic molecules [23] or achieved by the carbonization or pyrolysis of small organic molecule [24], but the processes are rather complicated and time-consuming. However, effective strategy for large-scale production of GQDs from renewable precursor with cheap and environmentally-friendly approaches remain difficult to achieve.

Thus far, as an environmentally-friendly, renewable, cheap, sustainable and natural carbon source, biomass can be used for mass production of GQDs [25]. Biomass (e.g., a range of plant leaves [14], grasses [26], orange peels [27], rice husks [28], coffee grounds [29] and wood charcoal [30]) are adopted for producing GQDs in yields comparable to expensive graphene-based precursors. Pineapple leaf fibers (PALFs), waste from the pineapple plant, are a highly underutilized tropical natural resource and a highly diverse biowaste residue in agricultural production. It has been reported that microcrystalline cellulose (MCC) from various natural biomass can serve as an excellent candidate to prepare nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) [31] and carbon dots (CDs) [32]. However, PALF, though abundant in cellulose, has never acted as a starting material to prepare GQDs.

In recent years, heavy metal ion pollution has caused significant problems globally due to its serious harm to human health and the environment. Mercury is a highly toxic heavy metal with toxicity and bioaccumulation, causing severe health effects even under significantly small concentration [11]. Mercury exposure can cause a variety of toxic effects (e.g., different cognitive and motor impairment, kidney failure, and brain damage) [11,33]. Dissolved mercury (II) ion (Hg2+) is one of the most stable forms of inorganic mercury, which is extremely toxic because of its good water solubility [34]. Under the serious environmental and health hazards of Hg2+, a rapid and simple method is required for Hg2+ detection with low cost, great sensitive property, and high selective property. Conventional Hg2+ detection approaches require complex sample preparation and sophisticated instrumentation, thereby limiting the relevant use for monitoring routine Hg2+ [11,35]. Thus, a simple method remains hard to be generated for detecting Hg2+ in aqueous solution. GQDs [7,9,10] and CDs [11,35-38] have been extensively explored as fluorescent probes to detect hazardous Hg2+. But, to our best knowledge, few groups have reported the detection of Hg2+ using GQDs derived from PALF.

Here, we reported a flexible and eco-friendly hydrothermal approach to produce water-soluble nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) with MCC extracted from PALF biomass as carbon source and ethylenediamine (EDA) as nitrogen dopant. The as-prepared N-GQDs , size around 3–8 nm, were thus found to possess better water solubility (about 1.0mg/mL), and exhibited excitation wavelength-dependent photoluminescence (PL). Unexpectedly, by controlling the pH value of the solution in the hydrothermal reaction, N-GQDs were able to exhibit green, cyan and blue light. Futhermore, N-GQDs could serve as a very effective fluorescent probe for the label-free, sensitive, and selective detection of Hg2+ by fluorescence quenching, which were successfully applied in real water samples. In addition, it could serve as a very effective fluorescent probe for the label-free, sensitive, and selective detection of Hg2+ by fluorescence quenching.The N-GQDs, could also be successfully applied to the determination of Hg2+ in real water samples.

PALFs were from pineapple plants (Haikou, Hainan province, China) with a fiber extraction machine, sodium hydroxide(NaOH), EDA, nitric acid (HNO3, 68%), sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98%) as well as hydrochloric acid (HCl, 36.5%). All solvents employed were analytical reagent grades. The solutions of Hg2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Cr3+, Fe3+, Co2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Ag+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Ba2+, and Na+ were prepared from Hg (NO3)2, Pb (NO3)2, CuCl2•2H2O, AlCl3, Cr (NO3)3•9H2O, FeCl3•6H2O, CoCl2, MnCl2, ZnCl2, FeCl2•4H2O, AgNO3, MgCl2, CdCl2, BaCl2 and NaCl, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MCC extraction and hydrothermal carbon synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: PALFs were dried in an oven at 105 ℃ for 24 h, and then ground into an approximate size of 2 mm. Fibers (10g) were mixed with 2 wt.% 200 mL aqueous NaOH solution at 80 ℃ with continuous mechanical stirring for 3 h. Next, the mixing with 3 wt.% HNO3 200 mL was conducted under the identical conditions. After mixed with 200 mL 5 wt.% HCl solution at 90 ℃ for 1.5 h, the powder was dispersed in 63.5% (w/w) H2SO4 solution over a time of 40 min to obtain MCC. After each time mixing with a solution, the specimens were cleaned with distilled water until pH reached neutrality, and then dried in the oven and ground into powder, while MCC were freeze-dried.

Hydrothermal carbon (HTC) was prepared from MCC. The reaction was performed in 50 mL Teflon lined stainless steel autoclaves filled with 45 mL 16 g/L MCC solution (under the 4 h continuing stirring) heating to 250 ℃ for 4 h. After being centrifuged at 5000 r/min, the resulting products received by washing with distilled water several times, then dried at 80 ℃ for 8 h.

25 mg dried solid HTC dispersed in 50 mL EDA solution (pH = 8, 10, 12, 14) string for 1 hour, and then performed in 25 mL Teflon lined stainless steel autoclaves at 200 ℃ for 10 h. The product containing water-soluble N-GQDs passed a 0.1 μm hydrophilic poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (PTFE) membrane for the removal of insoluble products. N-GQDs water solution was transferred to a dialysis bag which had been pretreated 20 min within boiling water, and further dialyzed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 1000 Da) for the removal of EDA. The deionized water was changed per 6 h in average to overall remove all impurities (e.g., inorganic salts) for nearly 72 h, which can be illustrated by the XRD pattern (Fig 3. a) and the FT-IR spectrum (Fig 3. b). Lastly, the preparation of the neutral aqueous solution of N-GQDs into a solid powder was obtained by freeze-drying.

Under 10 kV accelerating voltage, the size and morphology of PALF, MCC and HTC were characterized by Hitachi S3500N scanning electron microscope (SEM). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) image and TEM image of N-GQDs were collected on a NanoScope-IIIa MultiMode-AFM (Veeco, USA) and on a JEM-2100F electron microscope (JEOL Company, Japan) with the use of a 200 kV accelerating voltage, respectively. FT-IR analyses were carried out using a Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometer (TENSOR27, Bruker Optics; Germany); Spectra were acquired in a range of 400 to 4000 cm−1 through the performance of 16 scans at 4 cm−1 resolution. X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were recorded by means of X-ray diffractometer (D8 Advance, Bruker Optics; Germany) using Cu Kα radiation (X-ray tube voltage 30 kV; tube current 20 mA). This intensity was determined in a 2θ angular range between 5–60° with 0.02° step size in terms of the analyses. LabRAM HR 800 (Horiba Jobin Yvon, France) Raman spectrums were received on a Renishaw in plus laser Raman spectrometer with λex=633 nm; Q500-TGA (TA Company, USA) was utilized for the thermogravimetric analysis (TG) under an air flow rate of 20 ml/min, 20 ℃/min heating rate, with a temperature range from 40 ℃ to 700 ℃, With a Thermo Fisher ESCALAB 250Xi XPS spectrometer, X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were recorded. Overall fluorescence measuring processes were performed on an F-280 spectrofluorometer equipped with a xenon lamp source, and the speed for scanning was 1200 nm/min. The PL lifetime measurements were required on an FL-TCSPC fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon Inc, France). Lifetime experiments were obtained on an Edinburgh TSCPS FL 980.

Hg2+ detection was performed in deionized aqueous solution. In a typical test, different amounts of Hg2+ were added to 1 mL of a 0.1 g/L N-GQDs mixture and quickly mixed and diluted to 10 mL. After 10 min of reaction, PL spectrum was recorded. The selectivity for Hg2+ is determined through the introduction of other metal ion stock solutions instead of Hg2+ in a similar way. The recording for fluorescence spectrum based on 325 nm excitation, and the overall tests were done under 25 ℃ with 10 nm excitation wavelength slot.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the performance of N-GQDs in detecting Hg2+ in an artificial system, the water samples from Nandu River in Haikou were acquired. After filtration through a 0.20 μm filter membrane, the samples centrifuged at 10000 RPM for 10 min and then were spiked Hg2+ at different concentration levels. At present, Hg2+ are usually analyzed by atomic fluorescence spectroscopy. Therefore, for the accuracy requirements, both the standard method of atomic fluorescence spectroscopy (HJ 694 2014 [39]) and the proposed method were performed for detecting Hg2+ in actual samples.

The whole synthetic process was sketched in Fig. 1 . First, MCC were required from PALFs, which mainly based on acid hydrolysis-isolated. Second, the cellulose chains were hydrothermal decomposed into fructose, which subsequently decomposed to form organic acids [40]. The mentioned organic acids further catalyzed the reaction following degradation and finally formed solid HTC [41]. Third, N-GQDs were hydrothermally synthesized in different pH value aqueous solutions by using HTC as raw carbon material and EDA as nitrogen dopant. The cutting mechanism from HTC to GQDs should be related to the introduction of oxygen-containing groups, especially epoxy groups, onto the backbone and edges of the carbon structure during hydrothermal reactions [28]. When EDA existed in the reaction system, it not only formed some nitrogen-containing functional groups, but also introduced nitrogen atoms into the crystal lattice of carbon nucleus through hydrothermal process to obtain N-GQDs [42,43].

With the hydrothermal reaction under pH = 14 solution condition as a model, the morphologies and structures exhibited by the as-prepared N-GQDs were investigated. In the SEM image, the obtained MCC (Fig. 2 b) was separated into individual micro-sized fibers (10–15 μm in width) after PALFs (Fig. 2a) was chemically treated [44]. After the hydrothermal carbonization process, the HTC showed sphericity with a diameter less than 5 μm (Fig. 2c). Some agglomerations of the carbon particles were found based on the typical unmodified nanoparticle behavior [45]. Furthermore, the spherical morphology of the HTC, their size dispersity and aggregation tendency were demonstrated from the SEM image. The as-prepared N-GQDs powders could be redispersed in water with a high solubility of approximately 1.0 mg/mL, and the dispersion or solution continued to be homogeneous even after several months at room temperature with no aggregation or color change. The diameter and lateral parameter of N-GQDs were investigated with transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fig. 2d showed that the collected N-GQDs were monodisperse and had a relatively uniform diameter in the range of 3–8 nm. As indicated from the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image, the prepared N-GQDs exhibited high crystallinity and displayed a lattice spacing of 0.22 nm, corresponding to the lattice spacing of (100) facet of graphite [46,47]. The typical morphologic height of N-GQDs is shown in the atomic force microscope (AFM) image. Fig. 2f gives an AFM image of N-GQDs exhibiting a terrain height of 0.5-5.0 nm, with an average height of 3.7 nm, which demonstrated that most N-GQDs consisted of nearly 8-10 graphene layers. The mentioned results were consistent with the results of N-GQDs achieved by other carbon precursors in the literature.

The functional groups of MCC, HTC and N-GQDs were further identified by FT-IR (Fig. 3 a). 1735 cm−1 and 1509 cm−1 peaks disappeared in the FT-IR spectrum of MCC (Fig. 3a), making clear the removal of hemicelluloses and lignin after pretreatment [48]. The decrease (in relation to MCC) in the intensity of the HTC bands at 1000-1480 cm−1 and the broad band at 3000-3700 cm−1 suggested dehydration taken place in the cellulose's hydrothermal carbonization [49]. The peaks close to 2920, 2846 and 1394 cm−1 attributed to the -CH3 stretching of graphite decreased with the formation of N-GQDs. The presence of oxygen groups in the GQDs was suggested by the bands at 3000–3700 cm−1 (a wide band resulting from O-H stretching vibration in carboxyl or hydroxyl group), 1696 cm−1 (CO vibration representing carboxyl, ester, quinone or carbonyl) and 1056 cm−1 (O-H bending vibrations and C-O stretching vibrations in ether, ester or hydroxyl). Moreover, the peaks at 1642 cm−1, 1480 cm−1 belonged to the typical stretching modes of N-CO, heterocyclic C-N-C bonds [11,31]. From the above results it could be concluded that the N-containing groups were generated through chemical reaction between EDA and HTC, and N species were successfully introduced into GQDs. In addition, compared with the FT-IR spectrum of PLAF, MCC and HTC, the peak at 609 cm−1 disappeared in N-GODs, which was attributed to SO4 2- from Na2SO4 [50] and K2SO4 [51]. Thus, it was indicated that inorganic salts were completely removed after dialysis.

Besides, Fig. 3b illustrated the XRD patterns of PALF, MCC, HTC and N-GQDs. The XRD patterns of PALF and MCC showed obviously diffraction peaks at 16.5°, 22.4° and 34.7°, corresponding to [010], [002] and [040] planes of cellulose, respectively. The increased crystallinity of MCC was mainly due to the removal of lignin and hemicellulose in the amorphous region. The [002] peak of HTC and N-GQDs were both centered at 24.5° (d 002=0.36 nm) in the XRD patterns, [52], and the N-GQDs became broader, which meant the disordered structure increased in N-GQDs compared to HTC. This result was consistent with the result of Raman spectra (Fig. 3c). The weak peak at 41.5° (d 100=0.22 nm) and the [002] peak revealed an amorphous carbon phase and partial graphitization of N-GQDs, which were attributed to the existence of abundant functional groups and graphitization of carbon atom, respectively [31]. These results further confirmed that there was only graphitic carbon and no other crystalline phases, which was consistent with that of FT-IR in Fig. 3a. In addition, the mentioned result showed that the N-GQDs were obtained by the transformation of microcrystalline cellulose with EDA through the hydrothermal synthesis. [31]

On the whole, the relative intensity variation of the G and D bands in the Raman spectra generally revealed a change in the electronic conjugation state during preparation [53]. As depicted in Fig. 3c, the ratio of I D / I G of N-GQDs derived from HTC increased significantly, and the defect densities were determined to be 0.37 and 0.47, respectively. The introduction of oxygen-covering and N-containing functional groups led to higher I D / I G values of the N-GQDs than those of the HTC, which meant that the degree of defects on N-GQDs’ surface increased. Despite the increase in surface defects, the I D / I G values were still below 0.5, which were well below the values reported in the literature, indicating low defect density and high quality of N-GQDs derived from PALFs [4,7,15].

The distinctions of the chemical structures of HTC and N-GQDs were observed by their thermal resistance (Fig. 3d). The ignition temperature of HTC was at approximately 300 °C, and thermal resistance ranged from 300-500 °C. However, there were more than one initiation temperature of N-GQDs, thereby suggesting the presence of oxygen-containing functionalities in N-GQDs. Besides, N-GQDs had a broader range of thermal resistance (300-800 °C), which demonstrated that the hydrothermal reaction under pH=14 solution condition could acquire N-GQDs with a higher oxygen content, a higher heat resistance and a thermal stability. Furthermore, HTC curve showed no ash content, while the residual mass ratios were approximately 50% for N-GQDs. This might result from the presence of stable oxygen-containing and N-containing functional groups in N-GQDs [54].

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was selected to examine the variations in the chemical compositions of N-GQDs synthesis. The XPS survey spectrums (Fig. 4 a) showed intensity peaks at 284.3 eV, 399.4 eV and 537.1 eV represent C1s, N1s and O1s binding energies, separately [4,31]. Fig. 4b presented the high-resolution C1s spectra, and the spectra was deconvoluted into five peaks at about 290.7 eV (O-CO), 288.3 eV(CO), 286.3 eV(C–O), 285.1 eV(C–N) and 284.8 eV(C–C/C-H), respectively [4,31]. Table 1 listed the content of each component elements of HTC and N-GQDs. As expected, the process of generating N-GQDs from HTC was accompanied by C/O ratio reduction from 4.2 (HTC) to 2.9 (N-GQDs) as the content of oxygen-containing functional groups increased. The characterizations from FTIR and XPS clarified that the surface of the as-synthesized N-CQDs was functionalized by multiple oxygenated and nitrous groups.

For more insights to the effect of pH value changes on the optical properties of N-GQDs during hydrothermal synthesis, we studied the PL (Fig 5 A) and photoluminescence (PLE) spectra (Fig 5b) of samples synthesized at different pH values under the same concentration and neutral conditions. The PL and PLE showed the maximum excitation wavelength and emission wavelength positions of N-GQDs obtained at different pH conditions. The PL and PLE results showed that the normal fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs by hydrothermal under pH=14 EDA solution conditions was higher than that of the other three samples. As shown in Fig 5c, we could find that the emission bands blueshifted as the pH value of the EDA solution was controlled in the hydrothermal process, and the colors changed from green to blue, respectively, which can be more visually in Fig. 5c and Fig. 5d. This phenomenon can be explained by the reduction of non-radiative recombination centers reduction on the N-GQDs surface [55]. Upon such a mechanism, the collision of an oxygen molecule with an excited state quantum dots could contribute to a non-radiative energy transfer, thereby resulting in the decrease in the fluorescence emission intensity [31]. In the hydrothermal process, oxygen-containing groups (i.e., epoxy, ether, and carboxyl) produced as non-radiative recombination centers could reduce the fluorescence emission capability of N-GQDs. However, the existence of EDA could alter the groups of epoxy, ether and carboxyl into hydroxyl, which suppressed the non-radiative process and further enhance the integrity of π conjugated system as an electron donor [55]. In addition, amination and/or amidation created radiative recombination centers (e.g., amine (-C-N-) and amide groups (R2-NCOR)) belonging to the electrondonating groups. The mentioned electron-donating groups and π-electrons that existed in the -C=N group could enhance the fluorescence intensity of a substance [31]. Accordingly, the higher the concentration of the EDA solution (the higher the pH value), the stronger the fluorescence intensity of quantum dots would be. The CIE 1931 chromaticity coordinates changed as increasing pH value of the EDA solution during hydrothermal reaction process, thus indicating that N-GQDs photoluminescence characteristic was tunable through the introduction of oxygen-containing and N-containing functional groups at the periphery of N-GQDs. The PL spectra of the N-GQDs aqueous solution featured a strong emission peak at a range of 450 to 600 nm (Fig. 5e), which was dependent on the excitation wavelength (250−550 nm). As shown in Fig. 5f, the PL decay curves measured for N-GQDs could be fitted effectively by the mono-exponential function. The lifetimes of N-GQDs were 3.42 (pH=8), 3.59 (pH=10), 3.67 (pH=12) and 6.14 ns (pH=14), respectively, which were comparable with those previously reported [5,23,31]. Notably, N-GQDs at pH=14 achieved a significantly higher PL lifetime than that of the other three samples, indicating that the as-prepared N-GQDs under different pH conditions had a discrepant PL origin [56]. These results meant that the as-prepared N-GQDs exhibited unique optoelectronic property.

The high selectivity for Hg2+ Because of the high affinity of N to the surface of the quantum dots for the formation of complexes with Hg2+ , the N-GQDs exhibited high selectiveity for Hg 2+Because [7,9]. A competition experiment was performed for the investigation of the selectivity of the N-GQDs probe to Hg2+ with different metal ions. We introduced 50 μM of various metal ions related to biology and environment (e.g., Hg2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Cr3+, Fe3+, Co2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Ag+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Ba2+, and Na+) to the N-GQDs solution. Subsequently, the recording for PL response was conducted. Fig. 6 a showed that the selectivity and sensitivity of the probe towards Hg2+ were not affected to any extent by the presence of excess amounts of other metal ions, apart from slight interference from Cu2+ and Fe3+. With an increase in the concentration of Hg2+, the fluorescence intensity of the main absorption band (438 nm) of the N-GQDs tended to decline (Fig. 6c), and the fluorescence image of the N-GQDs aqueous solution turned darker under UV irradiation at 365 nm (Fig. 6d).

For the reconfirmation of the identification of Hg2+ by N-GQDs, the recovery property of the N-GQDs after Hg2+ detection had also been investigated by adding phosphate buffered saline (PBS) into the N-GQDs/Hg2+ solution. As observed above, the addition of Hg2+ could quench the fluorescence of the N-GQDs. Nevertheless, quenched PL was able to recover immediately upon the addition of PBS into the N-GQDs/Hg2+ solution. With rising PBS concentration, the PL almost restored to its original intensity (Fig. 6d). The result confirmed that the N-GQDs/Hg2+ sensing system was sensitive to PBS. Besides, adding PBS could turned “on” the quenched N-GQDs which was in the “off” state. Because of the formation of a strong Hg2+-PBS complex after the introduction of PBS, the inaccessibility of Hg2+ to N-GQDs made the N-GQDs keep free from Hg2+ [11,38]. All the results obviously elucidated the sensitive identification of Hg2+ by N-GQDs.

Finally, to further confirm the feasibility of using N-GQDs to detect Hg2+ in real environments, the accuracy and precision were calculated considering spiked river water samples at different concentrations. In the meantime, the standard method of atomic fluorescence spectroscopy (HJ 694 2014 [39]) was used for detecting Hg2+ in actual sample to meet the accuracy requirements. According to Table 3 , the recoveries of the spiked river water samples ranged from 89.3% to 107.1%, and the low RSDs, ranged from 2.64% to 9.25% when using N-GODs, which was similar to the standard method. The repeatability assessment results showed that this method was satisfactory and its recovery was within the range of environmental testing generally considered acceptable, the true value with % RSD≤20; The recovery value obtained here abide by the tolerance range of 70-130% of the standard method of HJ 694 2014 [39], as well. Good accuracy and precision revealed that the approach was feasible.

A water-soluble N-GQDs probe had been produced through an easy-to-use, green and cheap approach. The size of the as-prepared N-GQDs was about 0.37 nm, consist of about 8-10 graphene layers. Moreover, the N-GQDs exhibited prominent selectivity and sensitivity and were successfully applied for the quantitative detection of trace Hg2+ in aqueous solutions with the detection limit of 0.25 nM. The method is low-cost, simple and accurate that could be used for the routine analysis and measurement technique for trace Hg2+ in water environment.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge financial support from the Scientific Research Foundation of Higher Education Institutions, Hainan (Hnky2019-98). The authors appreciate the Analytical and Testing Center of Hainan University and Tianhe Research Analysis and Testing Center in terms of their technical supports and instruction.
